title: Poorly Animated Mouths Read Lips or Die
display_title: Poorly Animated Mouths Read Lips or Die
wikitext:
[]: Poorly Animated Mouths: Read Lips or Die             Quote Post by 0x2eleven » Sat Jun 05, 2010 4:37 am I'm aware that, to save money, characters mouths are often obscured throughout NGE to avoid having to animate them speaking. But I've noticed that there are times when a character's mouth will be fully visible and still not be moving while their voice is being heard and they are supposed to be speaking. Why is this? Usually when this happens, a still of the character with their mouth open or closed will run while all of the lines are delivered. An example of this can be seen in the scene in episode 06 when Rei tells a hospitalized Shinji about the upcoming operation against Ramiel. Does anyone know what this is about? Were scenes certain scenes really that poorly animated, or did something go wrong when the show was being transferred to digital media?

[]:             Quote Post by Dark doom » Sat Jun 05, 2010 5:10 am I think there simply miss-hap's where the artists slipped up. After all, those scene's could have been drawn before any voice acting had been recorded.

[]:             Quote Post by toe mash » Sat Jun 05, 2010 9:02 am In almost all anime, they record the voice actors before animating. It might just be a case of QUALITY. Also, what are you talking about in that example? Rei's mouth is fully obscured when she reads the description for their mission, and when it isn't her mouth is flapping properly. I actually don't remember cases in NGE where characters talked while their lips were static, do you have another example?

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sat Jun 05, 2010 2:19 pm I can only think of intended cases in Eva where this happened. Can you please provide us with some more examples?

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Sat Jun 05, 2010 3:00 pm In some of those shots the sheer quality keeps one from being too absorbed by the visuals. It seems very much akin to Kubrick's distancing effects with wide shots, except some really pivotal dialogue scenes are often delivered through them. And since I haven't done one in a while... I might just put together a cinematographic analysis just for the hell of it.

[]:             Quote Post by 0x2eleven » Sat Jun 05, 2010 4:33 pm Sachi wrote: I can only think of intended cases in Eva where this happened. Can you please provide us with some more examples?

[]:             Quote Post by Halicat » Sat Jun 05, 2010 5:31 pm I'd put it down to errors/cutting corners due to budget. Right now nothing comes off the top of my head save for moments during the last few episodes [Namely, Gendo asking Ritsuko why she destroyed the dummy plug system, such a still frame for an emotional outburst bothers me, she didn't even have a mouth at all! And Misato's eyes in the car mirror whilst discussing Kaworu with Hyuga not even being animated to do anything.] Though they make sense, considering they where at the very end of the series and with a low budget.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Sun Jun 06, 2010 4:30 am toe mash wrote: In almost all anime, they record the voice actors before animating.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Sun Jun 06, 2010 5:08 am It's still that way today from what I gather. Dubbing almost always comes after animation.

[]:             Quote Post by toe mash » Sun Jun 06, 2010 9:03 am NAveryW wrote: Incorrect. At the time NGE was made, animation was usually recorded first, then the seiyuu would record their lines together while watching the animation. You can see this in action in a couple of documentaries, including one for Mahoromatic , which shows that that was how Gainax did things. I'm not sure if it's still common practice or not, but lip sync is generally better these days.

[]:             Quote Post by Bomby von Bombsville » Sun Jun 06, 2010 11:09 am Usually in animation the dubbing is done before he animation, and recorded to the animatic. But with a TV schedule I could see them not having an animatic thanks to a tightened schedule.

[]:             Quote Post by Frozzy » Sun Jun 06, 2010 3:18 pm i never really looked but you mean why gendo always sat in his chair like this?

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Sun Jun 06, 2010 4:43 pm Because Gendo's a badass muthafucka who slaps around bitches and takes names like a ballot box. He also leiks to take off his gloves with his mouth and go "kid gloves are off all youz hoes."

[]:             Quote Post by toe mash » Mon Jun 07, 2010 3:05 am I think Nerv is a pretty cool guy. eh starts instrumentality and doesnt afraid of anything.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Mon Jun 07, 2010 4:06 am Bomby von Bombsville wrote: Usually in animation the dubbing is done before he animation, and recorded to the animatic.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Mon Jun 07, 2010 4:20 am Most of the time you'll have the seiyuu in a studio together so they sort of feed off of each other. Often I've seen the anime playing as they're dubbing the lines. They'll be watching it and record lines as it plays to get a sense of timing.

[]:             Quote Post by CazMu » Wed Jun 09, 2010 1:57 pm toe mash wrote: I think Nerv is a pretty cool guy. eh starts instrumentality and doesnt afraid of anything.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Wed Jun 09, 2010 2:04 pm Isn't the chick in your avatar named "Evangelion"?

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Wed Jun 09, 2010 2:06 pm CazMu wrote: Erm...his name is Gendo Ikari...not Nerv.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Wed Jun 09, 2010 2:30 pm This thread makes me wanna become an hero right now.

But I've noticed that there are times when a character's mouth will be fully visible and still not be moving while their voice is being heard and they are supposed to be speaking. Why is this?
